steps.label: What They Do
steps.body:
APIContext provides synthetic monitoring and conformance testing for APIs. They run scripted tests from real-world client locations and check both performance (latency, error rate) and correctness (schema, regulatory conformance — useful in financial services).

Different from RUM (which watches real users). Synthetic = continuous controlled tests, even when no users are around.

steps.label: How They Run on Akamai
steps.body:
APIContext launched synthetic test runners from 24 Akamai availability zones. Their backend uses Linode services across the board: LKE, StackScripts, NodeBalancers, Managed Databases, and Terraform for IaC.

Result: external API performance testing from real Akamai PoPs, mirroring where actual customers live.

steps.label: When To Position
steps.body: API-heavy enterprises: fintech, open banking, B2B SaaS, anyone with regulatory conformance requirements (PSD2, FAPI). Position alongside mPulse: mPulse for browser-side real users, APIContext for backend-API synthetic + conformance.
"APIContext = synthetic API testing from 24 Akamai zones. Pair with mPulse for full real + synthetic coverage."

quiz.question: A fintech needs continuous PSD2 conformance proof for their open-banking APIs. Which QCP?

options.explain: mPulse is browser-side RUM, not API conformance.

options.text: APIContext — synthetic + conformance testing from Akamai zones
options.explain: Designed for exactly this: continuous conformance against API specs and regulatory schemas.
Hydrolix observes; APIContext actively probes and validates schemas.

quiz.question: Synthetic vs. RUM — when do you need both?

options.text: Synthetic catches issues 24/7 even with no traffic; RUM tells you what real users experience
options.explain: Use both: synthetic for continuous SLA proof, RUM for real-world experience.

options.text: RUM is enough on its own
options.explain: RUM only fires when users hit you. Off-hours regressions slip through.

options.text: Synthetic is enough on its own
options.explain: Synthetic doesn't reflect actual user device/network mix.
